F-01.name: Ad Hominem
F-01.explanation: Attacking the person making the argument rather than addressing the argument itself.
F-01.example: You're just a young person who doesn't understand economics.

F-02.name: Straw Man
F-02.explanation: Misrepresenting someone's argument to make it easier to attack.
F-02.example: A: We should have better healthcare. B: Why do you want to give free stuff to everyone?

F-03.name: Appeal to Authority
F-03.explanation: Claiming something is true because an authority figure says so, even when they're not an expert in the relevant field.
F-03.example: A famous actor says this supplement works, so it must be effective.

F-04.name: False Dilemma
F-04.explanation: Presenting only two options when more exist.
F-04.example: You're either with us or against us.

F-05.name: Slippery Slope
F-05.explanation: Claiming that one event will inevitably lead to a chain of negative consequences without evidence.
F-05.example: If we allow students to redo exams, soon they'll stop taking tests altogether.

F-06.name: Circular Reasoning
F-06.explanation: The conclusion is included in the premise, making the argument tautological.
F-06.example: The Bible is true because it says so, and we know the Bible is true because it's the word of God.

F-07.name: Appeal to Popularity (Ad Populum)
F-07.explanation: Claiming something is true because many people believe it.
F-07.example: Millions of people smoke, so smoking must be safe.

F-08.name: Red Herring
F-08.explanation: Introducing an irrelevant topic to divert attention from the original issue.
F-08.example: Why worry about the environment when there are people starving?

F-09.name: Ad Verecundiam (Appeal to Ignorance)
F-09.explanation: Claiming something is true because it hasn't been proven false, or vice versa.
F-09.example: No one has proven aliens don't exist, so they must exist.

F-10.name: Hasty Generalization
F-10.explanation: Drawing a broad conclusion from a small or unrepresentative sample.
F-10.example: I met two rude people from that city, so everyone there must be rude.

F-11.name: Post Hoc Ergo Propter Hoc
F-11.explanation: Assuming that because B followed A, A caused B.
F-11.example: I wore my lucky socks and we won the game, so the socks caused the victory.

F-12.name: Appeal to Nature
F-12.explanation: Claiming something is good because it's natural, or bad because it's artificial.
F-12.example: This herbal remedy is safer because it's all-natural.

F-13.name: Tu Quoque
F-13.explanation: Deflecting criticism by pointing out the accuser does the same thing.
F-13.example: You say I shouldn't smoke, but you used to smoke too!

F-14.name: Genetic Fallacy
F-14.explanation: Judging something based on its origin rather than its current merits.
F-14.example: That theory was developed by a controversial person, so it must be wrong.

F-15.name: False Cause
F-15.explanation: Incorrectly identifying the cause of an event.
F-15.example: The rooster crows before sunrise, therefore the rooster causes sunrise.
